item.title: America at 250 — and the war it forgot
item.description: Native American nations were in a strong position before the 1794 Battle of Fallen Timbers

item.title: Putin could pay a personal price for failure in Ukraine
item.description: History suggests that military defeat often leads to radical political change in Moscow

item.title: AI and real GDP: how big and how fast?
item.description: Plus more on the chip cycle

item.title: FTSE 100’s likely new entrant puts a British spin on the AI boom
item.description: Hardware reseller’s trick will be to convince investors artificial intelligence can augment its services rather than replace them

item.title: The truth about the American profit machine
item.description: It’s not much stronger now than it was during the dotcom boom

item.title: How to bring nature into the home (beyond plants)
item.description: Design that is handmade, timeworn, sculptural, imperfect can introduce a sense of wildness within

item.title: Europe is running from a phantom China threat
item.description: EU leaders fear cheap Chinese exports when they should be worrying about embedded spyware and rare earths dependency

item.title: We need lifetime bans for clean athletes. This is the steroid Olympics, remember
item.description: A behind-the-scenes look at the work of Rutherford Hall, critical communications strategist

item.title: The end of cheap
item.description: After 50 years of falling capital, labour and energy prices, the next half-century will look very different for America

item.title: Africa’s response to Ebola must be defined by Africa itself
item.description: International support matters when it aligns with strategies built alongside regional institutions and governments

item.title: China’s comparative advantage is industrial policy
item.description: Western attempts to imitate Beijing’s state-funded model are unwise

item.title: England’s treatment of vulnerable children is a scandal
item.description: Care system is warped by incentives that put helpless young people at greater risk

item.title: Ferrari’s Luce is a free option on a whole new market
item.description: Italian company’s valuation stacks up even if it never figures out a workable electric vehicle strategy

item.title: There can only be one President Donald Trump, forever
item.description: By snubbing his son’s wedding he seemed intent on making sure Don Jr knows his place

item.title: The month of error and overwhelm
item.description: From international business events to school recitals, June schedules pile on more than we can handle

item.title: Dotcom bubble IPOs
item.description: Lessons for boomtime investing

item.title: The very British banality of the SNP’s shopping scandal
item.description: Seven Dyson vacuums, two Bremont watches . . . and a Jaguar in the driveway . . .

item.title: Five emerging themes for US stock investors
item.description: Active portfolios need to take into account factors such as risk management of giga-caps

item.title: No restaurant should be too posh to serve tap water
item.description: None of modernity’s silly purchases can compare with bottled water when there is drinkable stuff in the taps

item.title: The floor versus the ceiling
item.description: The world has come to prefer a high minimum to outright excellence

item.title: JPMorgan chases retail banking’s golden fleece
item.description: US bank’s international expansion is gaining some traction

item.title: Thanks to Airbnb, I’m dreading the summer in my garden
item.description: The spread of short-term rentals in British seaside towns is the end result of 50 years of unintended consequences

item.title: Britain is waiting too long to save vulnerable children
item.description: The scandal of profit-making residential homes is just the tip of the iceberg

item.title: Britain’s special relationship with French wine
item.description: Bordeaux — or claret — has long been at the heart of the British wine trade. But its spotlight is waning

item.title: ‘It aims at the delicious and restorative, then misses that target’ — Jay Rayner reviews Holy Carrot, London
item.description: Spitalfields’ latest vegetarian venue has a worthy premise, good-looking menu and a notable chef — and yet it’s falling flat
